label: short
chain_of_thought:
Okay, let's break this down. The user is asking whether a man can legally leave all his money to his current widow's sister, whom he plans to marry next year. First, I need to clarify the relationships involved.

The man is currently married to a woman (his wife). She has passed away, so she's his widow. The woman he's leaving the money to is his current widow's sister. He plans to marry this sister next year. So, he's leaving his money to his future wife, who is currently the sister of his deceased wife.

I need to consider the legal implications here. In many jurisdictions, there are laws about inheritance and testamentary capacity. The man is alive when he makes the will, so he can legally dispose of his assets as he sees fit, provided he has testamentary capacity (i.e., he's of sound mind and not under duress).

However, the question might be about whether this is allowed, but since the man is alive, he can make the will. The key point is that he's not married to the sister yet. So he's leaving money to someone who is not his current spouse (since his current spouse is deceased) but who is the sister of his deceased wife and a future spouse.

So, legally, can he leave money to a future spouse before the marriage? Yes, in most jurisdictions. The will is valid if made while he's alive and of sound mind. The fact that the beneficiary is going to be his future wife doesn't affect the validity of the will. The will can name anyone as a beneficiary, regardless of their current relationship status, as long as the will is properly executed.

But there might be some nuances. For example, if he marries this sister, then she becomes his wife. If he dies after the marriage, she would be his surviving spouse, and the will would still hold. If he dies before the marriage, then she is just a beneficiary named in his will. Since a will only takes effect upon the testator's death, and as long as the will is validly executed, the distribution of his assets can be to whomever he chooses.

Another consideration is whether there are any legal restrictions on marrying a sister-in-law, but that's a separate issue. The legality of the marriage itself might depend on local laws. In some places, marrying a sister-in-law (the sister of one's spouse) is allowed, but in others, it might be prohibited, especially if the deceased spouse is still legally married to the testator. However, since the testator is alive now and planning to marry the sister-in-law, the legality of the marriage would depend on local laws, but that's a different matter from the validity of the will.

So, in summary, the man can legally make a will leaving all his money to his current widow's sister, who he plans to marry next year, as long as he has testamentary capacity and the will is properly executed. The relationship between the testator and the beneficiary does not affect the validity of the will as long as the will is made while the testator is alive and the beneficiary is lawfully named.
